content:
Continuing from yesterday’s Morning Light, we saw how God gave the Gershonites two wagons and four oxen because their duty was to carry the coverings, curtains, hangings, and cords of the tabernacle. Their work looked like “cloth work,” but it was holy work, because it protected and formed the dwelling place of God in the camp. Today we will not go curtain by curtain, because each curtain has deep meaning and that belongs in our tabernacle lessons. For now, we will look at the curtains and coverings as one whole picture and learn what God is teaching us through them.

The coverings and curtains were not only for structure or decoration. They had a purpose. They covered the tabernacle, they separated spaces, and they protected what was holy. In the same way, God is teaching that our spiritual life also needs covering, separation, and protection. The tabernacle shows us what it means for God to dwell among His people, and the coverings show us how that dwelling is guarded.

The first meaning of covering is righteousness. After sin entered, man became exposed. In the beginning Adam and Eve were naked and felt no shame.

But after they sinned, they realized their nakedness and tried to cover themselves with leaves. That did not solve the problem. God Himself made coverings for them. This teaches a deep truth: man cannot cover himself.God must cover him. In the same way, the tabernacle was covered.Spiritually, this points to Messiah covering us with righteousness. We are not clothed by our goodness, but by His righteousness. Isaiah says, “He has clothed me with the garments of salvation, He has covered me with the robe of righteousness” (Isaiah 61:10). And Paul says, “For as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ” (Galatians 3:27). This means Christ is our covering. Without Him we are exposed. Without Him we stand in shame. But in Him we are clothed, accepted, and brought near.

The second meaning is separation. The curtains of the tabernacle created boundaries. They separated the outer court from the holy place, and the holy place from the most holy place. God was teaching His people that there must be a difference between what is holy and what is unholy. God’s presence is not approached in a careless way. Spiritually, this means our life must also be separated from sin. We cannot say we belong to God while we also love what defiles. Scripture speaks very plainly: “Come out from among them, and be separate, says Yahweh, and touch not the unclean thing, and I will receive you” (2 Corinthians 6:17). Separation is not pride. It is protection. It is not hatred of people. It is love for holiness. Just like the curtains marked the holy spaces, our choices must mark that we belong to God.

The third meaning is protection. The coverings protected the tabernacle from the sun, from dust, and from damage during travel. They shielded what was inside. Spiritually, this teaches that God protects His presence and what belongs to Him. God does not leave His dwelling place exposed. He covers it. He shelters it. Psalm 91 uses this same picture when it says, “He will cover you with His feathers, and under His wings you shall take refuge” (Psalm 91:4).

So the coverings remind us that God is not only the One who commands holiness. He is also the One who shields and keeps His people. When we stay under His covering, we are protected. When we step out into sin, we step out from under the shelter.

The fourth meaning is one of the most surprising. From the outside, the tabernacle did not look very attractive. It was covered with skins. It was not a shining palace on the outside. But inside there was gold, beauty, and glory.

This teaches a very important truth about God’s ways. True glory is often hidden from natural eyes. The world looks at outward appearance, but God looks at the heart. In the same way, a New Creation believer may not look impressive outwardly. Our life may look ordinary. We may not be celebrated. But inside, God is working transformation. Inside, there is a holy life being formed. Inside, there is the glory of Christ growing. This is why Scripture says our life is hidden with Christ in God (Colossians 3:3). The world may not see it, but God sees it, and in His time it will be revealed.

When we put all of this together, the coverings point to Christ. He is our righteousness, because He clothes us. He is our separation, because He brings us out of darkness into light. He is our protection, because we are kept under His care. He is the One who brings us into the presence of God. So the coverings teach us that God’s dwelling is guarded, and God’s people must also be guarded. We are not left exposed. We are covered. We are not left mixed with sin. We are separated. We are not left unprotected. We are sheltered.

And now the final line becomes very practical for daily life. These coverings were not only placed once and forgotten. They had to be carried again and again, every time the cloud moved. The same holy covering that protected God’s dwelling had to travel with the people through the wilderness. In the same way, our walk in Christ is not only one moment. It is a lifelong journey. We must carry this covering throughout our days. We must remain clothed in Christ’s righteousness. We must remain separated from sin. We must remain under God’s protection. That is why God provided the Gershonites with two wagons and four oxen. He gave strength for the journey, not only for one day. Tomorrow we will study what these wagons and oxen can teach us spiritually about carrying God’s covering through our whole life.

title: Prayer
text:
Yahweh and God our Father, thank You for covering us when we were exposed by sin.
Thank You for clothing us with righteousness through Christ, not by our works, but by Your grace.
Teach us to live separated from what is unclean, and to honor what is holy in our daily choices.
Keep us under Your shelter, and protect our hearts from the Sin and Shame of this world.
Help us not to seek outward glory, but to grow in the hidden inner work of Christ within us.
Strengthen us to carry this holy covering through every season, until our journey is complete.
In Christ name, amen.
